name: Ackee
description: Jamaica's national fruit and one of the most striking trees in the tropical landscape — brilliant red pods splitting open to reveal golden arils.

name: Bolivian Mangosteen
description: Rare lemondrop mangosteen — a truly exceptional find. Jewel-bright fruit with a citrusy-sweet flavor unlike anything at the grocery store.

name: Cacao
description: Grow your own chocolate. Cacao thrives in the dappled shade of a food forest and rewards patience with some of the most valuable pods on earth.

name: Mulberry
description: Fast-fruiting and generous — mulberry produces abundant sweet-tart berries while feeding birds and building soil beneath its canopy.

name: Soursop
description: Tropical fruit prized across the Pacific for its creamy, tangy flesh — a natural fit for Hawaiʻi's warm, wet climate.

name: Gliricidia
description: Madre de Cacao — a champion nitrogen-fixer and chop-and-drop mulch tree that feeds the whole system while shading young fruit trees.

name: Koki'o ke'o ke'o
description: A rare, fragrant white-flowering native hibiscus from Kauaʻi — seldom seen in cultivation and a standout in any native garden.

name: Kukui
description: The candlenut tree — Hawaiʻi's state tree. Fast-growing, culturally significant, and a generous provider of shade and living mulch.

name: Maʻo Hau Hele
description: Hawaiʻi's endangered state flower — a brilliant yellow native hibiscus and a meaningful addition to any restoration planting.

name: Sugar Cane
description: Two beloved heirloom varieties — Big Ribbon and Aina Kea — for chewing, juicing, or planting as a bold vertical accent.

name: Yacon
description: A Andean root vegetable that thrives in Hawaiʻi — crunchy, sweet, and low-glycemic. Digs itself out each season with no fuss.

name: Areca Palm
description: Graceful, clumping palm that creates instant tropical privacy. Thrives in full sun or dappled shade with little care once established.

name: Money Tree
description: The Malabar Chestnut, known across Asia as the Money Tree. Bold palmate leaves, edible seeds, and a reputation for good fortune.
